risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After confirming the diagnosis of uncomplicated AA by a CT scan, patients will be randomized by a closed envelope method either to undergo appendectomy or to receive antibiotic therapy with intravenous ertapenem. The randomization is performed in 1:1 equal allocation ratio. The 610 opaque, sealed, and sequentially numbered randomization envelopes are mixed and distributed to research hospitals..." Comment: The study describes a randomized allocation process using preprepared, mixed randomization envelopes in a 1:1 ratio, which provides sufficient context to judge that a random sequence was used.

Quote: "After confirming the diagnosis of uncomplicated AA by a CT scan, patients will be randomized by a closed envelope method either to undergo appendectomy or to receive antibiotic therapy with intravenous ertapenem... The 610 opaque, sealed, and sequentially numbered randomization envelopes are mixed and distributed to research hospitals by the main research center according to each hospital district population. To randomize a patient, an independent surgeon on duty will open the next consecutively numbered envelope." Comment: This explicitly describes sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelopes (SNOSE), which is an adequate allocation concealment mechanism preventing the enrolling clinician from knowing the upcoming assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The APPAC trial has been designed as a prospective randomized controlled, open label, non-inferiority multicenter trial..." and "The best design for a therapeutic trial is a randomized placebo-controlled, double-blind study, but with the interventions used in the APPAC trial the concealment would not be possible and therefore a randomized open design was chosen." Comment: Participants and treating personnel were explicitly not blinded. The study includes outcomes that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation, such as post-intervention pain scores (VAS 0–10), use of pain medication, sick leave, and aspects of clinical decision-making around treatment success/failure and recurrent appendicitis. Therefore performance bias is at high risk.

Quote: "The APPAC trial has been designed as a prospective randomized controlled, open label, non-inferiority multicenter trial..." and "At one week and two months pain score (VAS), possible additional need for sick leave, wound infections and recurrent AA will be registered." and "Patient outcome will be obtained during hospital stay (days 0, 1, 2) and then by a phone interview..." Comment: This was explicitly an open-label trial, so participants and treating clinicians/outcome assessors were not blinded. Outcomes included subjective and judgement-dependent measures: patient-reported pain scores (VAS) and sick leave, plus clinician-diagnosed wound infection and recurrent appendicitis diagnosed "on a clinical basis." Because at least one outcome was subjective/potentially influenced by knowledge of allocation, and patients themselves were assessors for pain during unblinded follow-up, the worst-outcome rule gives High risk for detection bias overall.

Summary: This publication is a trial design/protocol and does not report observed post-randomization attrition on any analysed outcome. It states only planned follow-up and an expected loss in the sample size calculation: "Patient outcome will be obtained during hospital stay (days 0, 1, 2) and then by a phone interview at one week, two months and at and one, three, five and ten years after the intervention." and "With an estimated 10 percent of the trial patients lost to follow-up, a maximum of 610 patients will be enrolled." Comment: Expected/planned loss to follow-up is not actual observed attrition. No per-arm randomized versus analysed numbers, reasons for missing outcome data, or analysed time-point denominators are reported, so risk of attrition bias for outcomes of interest cannot be judged as low or high from this paper.
